title: A Dual Metastable-State Encoding Architecture for Quantum Processing with $^{171}\mathrm{Yb}$ Atom Arrays
authors: Chun-Wei Liu, Saiwei Nie, Eesha Banerjee, Micah Davidson, Nick Reynolds, Alyssa L. Miller, Alex P. Burgers
abstract: Neutral-atom arrays combine scalable qubit registers, long coherence times, flexible optical control, and strong Rydberg-mediated entangling interactions, making them a promising platform for quantum information processing. However, physical error rates remain a challenge, and fault-tolerant quantum error correction (QEC) requires repeated mid-circuit measurement and reset of ancilla qubits without disturbing nearby data qubits. This requirement introduces significant control and architectural overhead, making qubit encoding an important architectural decision. Here, we propose a dual metastable-state qubit encoding for $^{171}\mathrm{Yb}$ atoms that utilizes two independent qubit subspaces in the $(6s6p)\,{}^3\mathrm{P}_0$ and $(6s6p)\,{}^3\mathrm{P}_2$ manifolds. The ${}^3\mathrm{P}_0$ manifold provides a long-coherence nuclear-spin (NS) qubit suitable for storage and arithmetic operations, while the ${}^3\mathrm{P}_2$ manifold provides a hyperfine-spin (HF) qubit, with $Δ_{\mathrm{HF}} = 2π\times 6.7~\mathrm{GHz}$, that enables fast Raman operations and direct state-selective imaging. Coherent shelving between the two metastable manifolds connects the qubit subspaces, allowing operations to be assigned to spectrally distinct processor zones. We simulate single-qubit and two-qubit gate fidelities in ${}^3\mathrm{P}_2$, as well as coherent shelving between the HF and NS qubit subspaces. We incorporate these physical-level estimates into an architectural resource estimation and logical-level simulation. Our approach integrates mid-circuit measurements and fast qubit operations within a single-species platform, providing a versatile framework for future fault-tolerant quantum computing with neutral-atom qubits.
summary: The paper proposes a novel dual metastable-state qubit encoding in $^{171}\mathrm{Yb}$ atoms to create a robust platform for quantum computation. By utilizing two distinct atomic manifolds ($3P_0$ and $3P_2$), the system can logically separate computational tasks into an ALU and a QEC block. This architecture enables high-fidelity, parallel operations necessary for scaling towards fault-tolerant quantum computing.
main_problem: The challenge is developing a scalable, fault-tolerant quantum processing architecture using neutral atoms that efficiently integrates mid-circuit measurements and fast qubit operations.
main_result: The proposed dual metastable-state encoding for $^{171}\mathrm{Yb}$ allows the system to partition into distinct Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) and Quantum Error Correction (QEC) blocks, enabling parallel operation and achieving high gate fidelities.
method: The authors simulate quantum dynamics using master equations and perform resource estimation by mapping logical circuits onto the physical hardware architecture.
model_or_system: The system uses $^{171}\mathrm{Yb}$ atoms trapped in optical tweezers, encoding qubits across two metastable manifolds: a long-coherence Nuclear Spin (NS) qubit in the $3P_0$ manifold and a fast Hyperfine (HF) qubit in the $3P_2$ manifold.
key_observables: Single-qubit gate fidelities ($F_{QB} \approx 99.9\%$), coherent shelving fidelity ($	ilde{F}_{CS} \approx 99.9\%$), and logical error rates as a function of code distance.
important_parameters: Hyperfine splitting $\Delta_{\mathrm{HF}} = 2\pi 	imes 6.7~\mathrm{GHz}$; coherence times up to $1.78$ s; two-qubit gate infidelity $<0.3\%$.
main_assumptions: The architecture assumes that separating the functional blocks (ALU/QEC) into distinct metastable manifolds minimizes latency associated with atom movement.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dual-metastable state encoding, the partitioning of the processor into ALU and QEC zones, and resource estimation comparing different scheduling strategies for syndrome extraction.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical platform and the dual-manifold encoding. It details gate operations (single-qubit gates in $3P_2$, coherent shelving between manifolds) followed by a comprehensive architectural analysis comparing resource usage and logical error rates based on how the two manifolds are assigned to computational roles.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it directly addresses the engineering challenge of building fault-tolerant quantum computers using neutral atoms, providing concrete architectural guidelines for integrating complex control sequences like mid-circuit measurement.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Dual-metastable state qubit encoding in a neutral atom processor. a. Qubits are encoded in the two metastable manifolds of 171Yb: hyperfine (HF) qubits in the (6s6p) 3P2 manifold and nuclear spin (NS) qubits in the (6s6p) 3P0 manifold. Both manifolds have single-photon coupling to the (6sns) 3S1 Rydberg manifold. The (6s7s) 3S1 manifold provides an intermediate state for single-qubit operations in the HF subspace and coherent shelving to the NS subspace. The (6s5ds)3D3 manifold provides a non-destructive readout channel via a 1798 nm cycling transition. b. Processor architecture based on this encoding: the arithmetic block utilizes NS qubits for algorithmic operations, and the QEC...

FIG. 2. Optical trapping and imaging in the 3P2 man- ifold. a. The readout of HF qubits is achieved by selectively imaging |0⟩HF through 3D3, F = 7/2, mF = −7/2 at 1798 nm. b. In 532 nm tweezers, the (6s5d) 3D3 manifold is anti- trapped, requiring a pulsed imaging protocol to avoid atom heating [43]. c. Additionally, we investigate trapping in 850 nm tweezers, where the 3D3 manifold is trapped. The imaging process can be interleaved with polarization gradient cooling (PGC) to compensate for heating due to the differential trap depth between 3P2 and 3D3. d-e. We estimate the coherence time of HF qubits (initialized in |0⟩HF) to be T1 = 1.78 s in 532 nm tweezers and T1 = 1.44 s in 850 nm...

FIG. 3. Single-qubit operation of 3P2 hyperfine qubits. a. Level structure and Raman coupling scheme for imple- menting single-qubit operations within the 3P2 manifold. The qubit states |0⟩HF and |1⟩HF are coupled via the intermediate 3S1, F ′ = 3/2, mF ′ = −3/2 state using a two-photon Raman transition. b. Scattering error as a function of the two-photon de- tuning ∆QB with fixed Raman beam powers. To calculate the error, we include Raman and Rayleigh scattering, differential light shifts, and Doppler effects (See Appendix B 1). The inset shows the corresponding Rabi oscillations for ∆QB = 2π×20 GHz and ΩQB = 2π × 2 MHz. We select an operating point that balances scattering error and...

FIG. 4. Coherent shelving between 3P2 and 3P0 manifolds. a. Level structure and Raman coupling scheme used for the coherent shelving (CS) process. The two Raman pathways, CS|0⟩and CS|1⟩, are modeled using a pair of 770 nm beams detuned by ∆HF = 2π × 6.7 GHz, with an additional nuclear-spin splitting ∆N = 2π × 11.5 kHz, and a single 649 nm beam under a magnetic field B = 10 G. These pathways coherently couple |0⟩HF ↔|0⟩NS and |1⟩HF ↔|1⟩NS via the 3S1 manifold. b. The scattering error as a function of the two-photon detuning ∆CS is computed for a fixed Raman beam power, which yields the Rabi frequency ΩCS = Ω|0⟩ CS = Ω|1⟩ CS = 2π × 2 MHz at ∆CS = 2π × 20 GHz (Detailed in Appendix B 1). Insets...

FIG. 5. Implementing CZ gate in the 3P2 manifold. a. We selectively couple |1⟩HF = 3P2, F = 3/2, mF = −3/2 to the Rydberg state |r⟩ ≡ |v = 54.28, L = 0, F = 1/2, mF = −1/2⟩with a σ+ polarized beam at 326 nm and assume the interatomic spacing to be 2.5 µm. The estimated interaction energy at this spacing is V = h × 100 MHz [69]. b. The time-optimal phase gate design (brown solid line) is modeled assuming a smooth-step amplitude function (purple solid line) with peak Rabi fre- quency Ωr = 2π × 1.5 MHz.
